title: Environment Variables and Configuration
description: Manage configuration securely using environment variables and dotenv.

slides.title: Why Environment Variables? 🔐
slides.content:
# Twelve-Factor App – Config

In production, configuration (database passwords, API keys, ports) should be **separated from code**. Storing secrets in code is a security risk and makes it hard to change config per environment.

Environment variables are the standard solution. They are set outside the application and accessed via `process.env`.

Example:
```javascript
const port = process.env.PORT || 3000;
```

slides.title: Using dotenv for Local Development 📝
slides.content:
## dotenv

During development, you can store environment variables in a `.env` file and load them using the `dotenv` package.

Install:
```bash
npm install dotenv
```

At the top of your main file:
```javascript
require('dotenv').config();
```

Create a `.env` file (never commit it!):
```
PORT=3000
DB_URL=mongodb://localhost:27017/myapp
JWT_SECRET=mysecret
```

Now `process.env.PORT` etc. are available.

slides.title: ⚠️ Never Commit .env
slides.content:
## Keep Secrets Secret

Always add `.env` to your `.gitignore` file. Instead, provide a `.env.example` file with dummy values so other developers know which variables are needed.

For production, set environment variables directly in your hosting platform (Heroku config vars, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.).

quizData.question: How do you access an environment variable named `DB_PASSWORD` in Node.js?
